summary: Martin draws a vivid parallel between TV hoarders who fill their homes with junk until they become unlivable, and programmers who accumulate cruft until codebases become unmaintainable 'legacy code.' He argues that the hoarder mentality—believing new acquisition is more valuable than organization, and eventually giving up because the mess seems insurmountable—mirrors how developers prioritize new features over cleanup. The post challenges the notion that legacy code is inevitable, arguing instead that professionals should leave code better than they found it, with quality improving over time rather than degrading.
key_points: The hoarder mentality believes acquiring new things (features) is more valuable than organizing existing things (refactoring)
Once mess accumulates, people rationalize that cleanup is pointless because no effort could 'make a dent'
Legacy code is the programmer's equivalent of a hoarder's home—a mess left for others to clean up
True legacies improve with age; professionals make their work better over time, not worse
The question isn't whether legacy code exists, but whether you're creating value or just building a hoard for others to clean up
money_quotes: Do you wander through dark passageways of floor to ceiling cruft as you attempt to add some new feature or fix some old bug?
We have a name for the result. We call it Legacy Code. The very term fills us with disgust and despair.
The legacy that a Hoarder leaves is a legacy of junk, trash, filth, and the huge effort required to get it all cleaned up.
A true legacy gets better with age.
Can you imagine hiring an artist, a mechanic, an engineer, a doctor, a lawyer, or anyone else, whose work products get worse the longer they work on them?
A professional team of programmers make their code better each day. The quality of the code improves with time. That's how you know they are professionals.
Is your work leaving a legacy of value, or are you just building a monstrous hoard for someone else to clean up?
key_insight: Professional programmers leave code better than they found it—if your codebase gets worse over time, you're not building a legacy, you're hoarding.
